title: Unshriven
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! the sun rose on the lea, and the bird sang merrilie,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the steed stood ready harnessן¿½d in the hall,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he left his ladyן¿½s bower, and he sought the eastern tower,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he lifted cloak and weapon from the wall.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½We were wed but yester-noon, must we separate so soon?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Must you travel unassoiled and, aye, unshriven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With the blood stain on your hand, and the red streak on your brand,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And your guilt all unconfessed and unforgiven?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Thoן¿½ it were but yester-even we were wedded, still unshriven,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Across the moor this morning I must ride;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I must gallop fast and straight, for my errand will not wait;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fear naught, I shall return at eventide.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½If I fear, it is for thee, thy weal is dear to me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yon moor with retribution seemeth rife;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As weן¿½ve sown so must we reap, and Iן¿½ve started in my sleep
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At the voice of the avenger, ן¿½Life for lifeן¿½.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½My arm is strong, I ween, and my trusty blade is keen,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the courser that I ride is swift and sure,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I cannot break my oath, though to leave thee I am loth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There is one that I must meet upon the moor.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! the sun shone on the lea, and the bird sang merrilie,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Down the avenue and through the iron gate,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Spurrן¿½d and belted, so he rode, steel to draw and steel to goad,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And across the moor he galloped fast and straight.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! the sun shone on the lea, and the bird sang full of glee,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ere the mists of evening gatherן¿½d chill and grey;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the wild birdן¿½s merry note on the deaf ear never smote,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the sunshine never warmed the lifeless clay.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ere the sun began to droop, or the mist began to stoop,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The youthful bride lay swooning in the hall;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Empty saddle on his back, broken bridle hanging slack,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The steed returned full gallop to the stall.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! the sun sank in the sea, and the wind wailed drearilie;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let the bells in yonder monastery toll,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For the night rack nestles dark round the body stiff and stark,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And unshriven to its Maker flies the soul.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Adam Lindsay Gordon represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Adam Lindsay Gordon's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
